title: SOMETHING BLUE: THE SAPPHIRE ENGAGEMENT RING RENAISSANCE
description: Move over diamonds — sapphires are having their most regal moment yet. Here are the stones and settings worth waiting for.
content:
Princess Diana's sapphire-and-diamond cluster ring arrived at a moment when engagement jewellery had grown formulaic. The diamond solitaire, beautiful as it undeniably is, had come to represent not so much love as compliance — a social expectation to be met rather than a personal statement to be made. The sapphire changed all of that.

When Catherine, Princess of Wales, accepted the same ring decades later, the sapphire engagement ring moved from historical curiosity to contemporary aspiration. The waiting lists at jewellers from Hatton Garden to the Place Vendôme confirmed what the designers already suspected: there was a tremendous appetite for something different.

What makes the sapphire so compelling is the breadth of its possibilities. The stone comes in virtually every hue — the Ceylon blues that range from pale sky to deep midnight; the padparadscha, named for the lotus flower, in apricot and pink; the Yogo gulch sapphires of Montana with their violetish blue; the vivid yellows from Sri Lanka that sit like trapped sunlight.

The new generation of sapphire engagement rings being shown by the great houses moves beyond the traditional cluster setting into territory that feels genuinely contemporary. Bezel-set stones in oxidised silver. East-west orientations that play with the stone's elongated geometry. Mixed-metal settings that allow the sapphire to read differently in artificial light than in natural.

The sapphire does not compete with the diamond. It simply offers something the diamond cannot: variety, history, and the particular pleasure of a colour that changes its character with the light.
keywords: sapphire, engagement rings, fine jewellery, coloured gemstones, luxury, Princess Diana
